request.question: Does Empire of the Sun or 3 Doors Down have more band members?

title: Empire of the Sun (band)
Bigger Than Life Tour
3 Doors Down (album)
Citizen/Soldier
Away from the Sun
3 Doors Down
Here Without You
When I'm Gone (3 Doors Down song)
The Doors discography
Seventeen Days
sentences: Empire of the Sun is an Australian electronic music duo from Sydney, formed in 2007.
The band is a collaboration between Luke Steele of alternative rock act The Sleepy Jackson, and Nick Littlemore of electronic dance outfit Pnau.
Their 2008 debut album "Walking on a Dream" brought the duo international success and has been certified double platinum in Australia and gold in the United Kingdom.

The album provided a number of internationally charting singles including "Walking on a Dream" which peaked at number ten on the Australian ARIA Singles Chart and reached number sixty four on the UK Singles Chart, and "We Are the People", which peaked at number fourteen on the UK Singles Chart.
The Bigger Than Life Tour was a co- headlining tour by American rock bands, Daughtry and 3 Doors Down.
The tour supported Daughtry's third studio album, "Break the Spell", and 3 Doors Down's "The Greatest Hits" album.
3 Doors Down is the self-titled fourth studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on May 20, 2008. "
It's Not My Time" was the first single from the album and was released in February 2008.
It can be heard on the band's MySpace site.
Another song on the album, "Citizen/Soldier" was released in 2007 as a tribute to the National Guard.
"Citizen/Soldier" is a single by the American alternative rock band 3 Doors Down from their self-titled album "3 Doors Down".
The song was released as a single in November 2007 in conjunction with a recruitment campaign by the United States National Guard.
The lyrics convey the band's views regarding the actions performed by the Guard.
It was released as the third single for American active rock and mainstream rock radio stations in November 2008, while it is their fourth overall single.
Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.
The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".
The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.
Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".
The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.
3 Doors Down is an American rock band from Escatawpa, Mississippi, that formed in 1996.
The band originally consisted of Brad Arnold (lead vocals/drums), Todd Harrell (bass), and Matt Roberts (lead guitar, backing vocals).
They were soon joined by rhythm guitarist Chris Henderson.
The band rose to international fame with their first single, "Kryptonite", which charted in the top three on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
The band then signed with Republic Records and released their debut album, "The Better Life", in 2000.
The album was the 11th-best-selling album of the year and was certified 6x platinum in the United States.
They were later joined by drummer Richard Liles, who played during the tour for their first album.
"Here Without You" is a song by American rock band 3 Doors Down.
It was released in August 2003 as the third single from the album "Away from the Sun".
It peaked at No. 5 on the "Billboard" Hot 100, for the week ending November 8, 2003.
Only their songs "Kryptonite" and "When I'm Gone" reached higher positions on the chart, peaking at No. 3 and No. 4, respectively.
It has since been certified 2x Platinum in the United States and Platinum in Australia.
The song was a large success on pop radio, becoming their third No. 1 hit on the Mainstream Top 40 chart and their first No. 1 on the Adult Top 40 chart, where it stayed at the top for 13 weeks.
"When I'm Gone" is a song by American rock band 3 Doors Down.
It was released in October 2002 as the lead single from their second album, "Away from the Sun".
It peaked at  4 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 chart in April 2003.
The song also spent seventeen weeks atop the "Billboard" Hot Mainstream Rock Tracks chart from November 2002 through March 2003, becoming one of the longest-running number-one singles on that chart.
The song also peaked  2 on the Hot Modern Rock Tracks chart almost reaching  1 by one spot from Queens of the Stone Age's "No One Knows" on the issue dated February 15, 2003.
It spent one week atop the Top 40 Mainstream chart for one week on May 3, 2003 and also peaked at  3 on the Adult Top 40 chart.
The following is the discography of the American rock band The Doors.
Formed in Los Angeles in 1965, the group consisted of Jim Morrison (vocals), Ray Manzarek (keyboards), John Densmore (drums), and Robby Krieger (guitar).
The Doors became one of the most popular rock bands of their era.
Their debut album, "The Doors" (1967), released by Elektra Records, charted at No. 2 on the US "Billboard" 200 and produced the group's most successful single, "Light My Fire".

The album received several sales certifications including a 4 times multi-platinum from both the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA), and from the Canadian Recording Industry Association (CRIA).

The Doors' second studio album, "Strange Days" (1967), often recognized as their most creative output, failed to produce a hit single as popular as "Light My Fire", though the album sold well commercially but did not reach the same level of success as the debut.
It reached No. 3 on the "Billboard" 200 and was certified platinum in the United States and Canada.
The Doors' third studio album "Waiting for the Sun" (1968), was regarded as an artistic disappointment when compared to their earlier material.
However, commercially it was very successful and reached No. 1 in the US and France, and produced their second No. 1 single, "Hello, I Love You".
"Waiting for the Sun" was the first Doors album to chart in the United Kingdom, where it peaked inside the Top 20.
The album was certified gold in that country by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI), as well as being certified gold and platinum in several other countries.
Seventeen Days is the third studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on February 8, 2005, five years to the day that 3 Doors Down released their debut album, "The Better Life".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- If summary_1 already includes explicit profession/role/descriptor tokens for an anchor (for example, 'singer', 'director', 'country music artist'), omit those redundant descriptor tokens and emit a compact anchor-only query consisting of the core visible anchors (names, titles, groups). Retain relation tokens only when the question explicitly requests them or when disambiguation rules demand them. Do not remove descriptors when the question explicitly asks for role/occupation evidence or when a short-title disambiguation rule requires adding an entertainment disambiguator.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity (or otherwise names candidate/bridge entities), treat the context claim as the summary anchor and perform explicit identity-alignment checks before using passage facts. For each entity explicitly named in `context` as a candidate or bridge, do the following: if at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append a single brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that linking fact and references the corroborating passage and also include one short clause for that context-named entity giving the passage-supported relation value (e.g., "Name: [relation value]."); if no passage provides the relation value for that context-named entity, append a short clause stating that no relation value is provided in the evidence (e.g., "Name: no [relation] provided in evidence."). Do not create such clauses for entities that are only mentioned in `passages` and are not named in `context`. Do not append corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep these additions short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.
- Vocal-role single-token normalization (narrow scope): If summary_2 (alone or composed with summary_1 where identity links are explicit) explicitly contains vocal-role evidence — i.e., one or more verbalized vocal-role phrases that clearly denote a singing performer (examples: "lead singer", "lead vocals", "vocalist" as presented in the summaries) — then you may prefer to emit the concise single-token common noun for a person who sings (e.g., "singer") only when that single-token common noun itself appears verbatim in the summaries or when the vocal-role phrases in the summaries unambiguously denote the same person-role. If those explicit lexical licenses are not present, retain the literal role phrasing as given in summary_2 and do not normalize modifiers or drop qualifiers.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Does Empire of the Sun or 3 Doors Down have more band members?
hop1_docs: Another 700 Miles | Another 700 Miles is a live EP by 3 Doors Down released on November 11, 2003.  It was recorded live at a performance in Chicago, Illinois during the band's Away from the Sun tour.

High Tyde | High Tyde are an English indie pop quartet from Brighton, England.  The ages of the band members range from 18 to 19.  They have played at major music festivals, such as Boardmasters, Dot 2 Dot, Underground, Fieldview, Reading, and Y Not.  They have also played support shows for Little Comets, Bad Suns, Young Kato, and Peace.  They have been featured on BBC Radio 1.  The sound of the band is inspired from indie bands like Two Door Cinema Club and Foals.  Their sound also has similarities of bands like The 1975 and Bombay Bicycle Club.  Their music gives off a summer vibe.

Away from the Sun | Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.  The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".  The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.  Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".  The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.

3 Doors Down (album) | 3 Doors Down is the self-titled fourth studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on May 20, 2008. " It's Not My Time" was the first single from the album and was released in February 2008.  It can be heard on the band's MySpace site.  Another song on the album, "Citizen/Soldier" was released in 2007 as a tribute to the National Guard.

The Road I'm On | "The Road I'm On" is a song recorded by American rock band 3 Doors Down.  It was released in April 2003 as the second single from their second album, "Away from the Sun".  The song reached number 8 on the U.S. "Billboard" Mainstream Rock charts and number 24 on the U.S. "Billboard" Modern Rock charts.

Supralateral arc | A supralateral arc is a comparatively rare member of the halo family which in its complete form appears as a large, faintly rainbow-colored band in a wide arc above the sun and appearing to encircle it, at about twice the distance as the familiar 22° halo.  In reality, however, the supralateral arc does not form a circle and never reaches below the sun.  When present, the supralateral arc touches the (much more common) circumzenithal arc from below.  As in all colored halos, the arc has its red side directed towards the sun, its blue part away from it.

Walking on a Dream (song) | "Walking on a Dream" is a song by Australian electronic music duo Empire of the Sun, which was released on 30 August 2008 as the lead single ahead of their debut album of the same name (3 October 2008).  The track was co-written by band members, Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore with Jonathan "Donnie" Sloan.  The full Sam La More remix was made available as a free download on the band's official website.
response.summary_1: Evidence: Passage 7 calls Empire of the Sun an "Australian electronic music duo" and names "band members, Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore." Passages 1, 3–5 refer to "3 Doors Down" as an "American rock band" but do not list members or a member count. Missing/next hop: retrieve the 3 Doors Down article (infobox or "Members"/"Current members" section) to get the number of band members; also retrieve the Empire of the Sun band article or infobox to confirm it is a duo. Useful anchors to search: "Empire of the Sun 'Australian electronic music duo' 'Luke Steele' 'Nick Littlemore' 'members'", and "3 Doors Down 'American rock band' 'members' 'current members' 'band members'".
response.hop2_query: Empire of the Sun members 3 Doors Down members
hop2_docs: The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up) | "The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up)" is a single released in 1983 by the musical group Level 42.  It is one of the few songs by the group which featured Mike Lindup on lead vocals (although Mark King did contribute as lead vocalist on this track).  After the success of "The Chinese Way", and of the album "The Pursuit of Accidents", this song featured on the newly released "Standing in the Light" album.  The first single from the album, "Out Of Sight, Out Of Mind", did not obtain the expected success for the recording and for the members of the band, leaving the whole expectation for the second single, The Sun Goes Down.

Two Door Cinema Club | Two Door Cinema Club are an Irish indie rock band from Bangor and Donaghadee in County Down, Northern Ireland.  The band formed in 2007 and is composed of three members: Alex Trimble (vocals, rhythm guitar, beats, synths), Sam Halliday (lead guitar, backing vocals), and Kevin Baird (bass, synths, backing vocals).
Another 700 Miles | Another 700 Miles is a live EP by 3 Doors Down released on November 11, 2003.  It was recorded live at a performance in Chicago, Illinois during the band's Away from the Sun tour.

Passiena (gens) | The gens Passiena, occasionally written Passienia, Passenia, Passennia, or Passenna, was a plebeian family at Rome, originally of equestrian rank, but at least one member was later admitted to the patriciate.  Members of this gens appear in history from the early years of the Empire down to the third century, and several obtained the consulship, beginning with Lucius Passienus Rufus in 4 BC.

Luke Steele (musician) | Luke James Steele (born 13 December 1979) is an Australian musician, singer and songwriter.  Steele is the vocalist and primary songwriter of the alternative rock band The Sleepy Jackson and is a member of the electronic music duo Empire of the Sun.

Away from the Sun | Away from the Sun is the second studio album by American rock band 3 Doors Down, released on November 12, 2002.  The singles released for the album were as follows: "When I'm Gone", "The Road I'm On", "Here Without You" and "Away from the Sun".  The recording sessions took place during the summer of 2002 with Producer/Engineer Rick Parashar at London Bridge Studios, in Seattle, Washington.  Rush guitarist Alex Lifeson performed live with the band during their premiere party in Biloxi for "Away from the Sun".  The album has sold four million copies worldwide, including well over three million in the US alone.

Jean-Pierre Door | Jean-Pierre Door (born April 1, 1942 in Sully-sur-Loire) is a member of the National Assembly of France.  He represents the Loiret department, and is a member of the The Republicans.
hop2_titles: The Sun Goes Down (Living It Up)
response.summary_2: Empire of the Sun is an Australian electronic music duo (context names Luke Steele and Nick Littlemore). Passage 5 explicitly corroborates Luke Steele as a member of that duo (Luke Steele: duo member); Nick Littlemore: no member evidence in the supplied passages. 3 Doors Down: no member count provided in the passages, so the evidence does not determine which band has more members.
response.answer: the evidence does not determine which band has more members.
